rulings.Case Title: WIEGEL LAW GROUP, PLC VS. LARRY ODBERT
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION - Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 1, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER WIEGEL LAW GROUP, PLC MOTION - Motion For Judgment On Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award. Petitioner Wiegel Law Group, PLC's motion for judgment on petition to confirm arbitration award is granted. Respondent Larry Odbert did not properly file or notice for hearing his purported cross-petition to correct contractual arbitration award for hearing. However, it raises the same issues as those in his opposition to petitioner's motion, and is denied. Respondent fails to show that the arbitrator exceeded her powers within the meaning of Code Civ. Proc. §§ 1286.2(a)(4) or 1286.6(b). Even if the arbitrator misinterpreted the parties' agreement, to the extent this simply constitutes an error of law, it is not a ground to vacate the award. (See Gueyffier v. Ann Summers, Ltd. (2008) 43 Cal.3d 1179, 1184 ["When parties contract to resolve their disputes by private arbitration, their agreement ordinarily contemplates that the arbitrator will have the power to decide any question of contract interpretation, historical fact or general law necessary, in the arbitrator's understanding of the case, to reach a decision. [Citations.] Inherent in that power is the possibility the arbitrator may err in deciding some aspect of the case. Arbitrators do not ordinarily exceed their contractually created powers simply by reaching an erroneous conclusion on a contested issue of law or fact, and arbitral awards may not ordinarily be vacated because of such error, for '"[t]he arbitrator's resolution of these issues is what the parties bargained for in the arbitration.'""]; Moncharsh v. Heily & Blase (1992) 3 Cal.4th 1, 11 ["[I]t is the general rule that, with narrow exceptions, an arbitrator's decision cannot be reviewed for errors of fact or law."].) Moreover, the arbitration award reveals on its face that the arbitrator awarded interest on the fees at the rate of 12% simple interest, the rate of interest called for by the parties' agreement, which does not exceed the legal rate authorized by Civil Code § 1916.12-1. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for petitioner is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and a proposed judgment which recites all the terms of relief made in the arbitration award and must bring them to the hearing or email them to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(EPS/302)

rulings.Case Title: SAMUEL JACKSON VS. SBS CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 1, 2019, Line 5, CROSS DEFENDANT FIVE STAR FLOORS, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication. Cross-defendant Five Star Floors, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment or summary adjudication as to SBS Corporation's cross-complaint is denied. Five Star failed to maintain its initial burden of production. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 437c(p)(2).) The lynchpin of Five Star's motion is that it was performing its work in the kitchen area and the accident occurred at a different portion of the worksite. Five Star relies on 184:4-7 of plaintiff's deposition to show that it was working in the kitchen. (Five Star's UMF 15.) Five Star mischaracterizes plaintiff's testimony. Plaintiff merely testified that Five Star started bringing flooring material to the kitchen. The record is thus unclear precisely where Five Star was performing its services. The court sustains SBS's objection to the declaration of Eric Ross as lacking foundation. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 437c(d) ["Supporting and opposing affidavits or declarations shall be made by a person on personal knowledge, shall set forth admissible evidence, and shall show affirmatively that the affiant is competent to testify to the matters stated in the affidavits or declarations."]; Evid. Code § 702(a) ["the testimony of a witness concerning a particular matter is inadmissible unless he has personal knowledge of the matter."]; Mamou v. Trendwest Resorts, Inc. (2008) 165 Cal.App.4th 686, 692 fn.1 ["bald recital" that contents of declaration are based on declarant's personal knowledge cannot "satisfy the proponent's burden to affirmatively demonstrate that the witness is testifying from his own perception of the events he describes."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for SBS is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(EPS/302)

rulings.Case Title: KAREN CLOPTON VS. CALIFORNIA PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 1, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION, MICHAEL PICKER, LIANE RANDOLPH MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. Defendants' motion for summary adjudication is denied. Under Code of Civil Procedure § 437c(f)(1), "a motion for summary adjudication shall be granted only if it completely disposes of a cause of action." A cause of action has no merit if "a defendant establishes an affirmative defense to that cause of action." (Code Civ. Proc. § 437c(o)(2).) A defendant has met that burden if she shows that "there is a complete defense to the cause of action." (Id. § 437c(p)(2).) Defendants here do not meet this burden. They bring their motion based on collateral estoppel, arguing that Plaintiff is "estopped from litigating her termination in this action" by a decision of the State Personnel Board upholding her dismissal. However, the issue of Plaintiff's termination does not comprise any entire cause of action. Rather, termination is but one of 11 separate adverse employment actions on which Plaintiff bases each of her four causes of action. (1st Am. Compl. 31-32, 38-45.) Defendants acknowledge all the other alleged adverse actions in their motion and admit that Plaintiff's allegations do "concern alleged adverse actions aside from the termination," although they attempt to minimize their significance by qualifying them as "a very small portion of Plaintiff's allegations." (Defs.' Memo. at 1-2.) Later, Defendants even state that Plaintiff may maintain her cause of action under the Whistleblower Protection Act (first claim for relief) but is collaterally estopped from basing it on her termination. (Defs.' Memo. at 7.) Defendants fail to establish that they have a complete defense to any of Plaintiff's causes of action. (See McCaskey v. California State Automobile Assn. (2010) 189 Cal.App.4th 947, 975 ["If a cause of action is not shown to be barred in its entirety, no order for summary judgment-or adjudication-can be entered."] [trial court erred in granting summary adjudication of FEHA claims on statute of limitations grounds]; Nazir v. United Airlines, Inc. (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 243, 251 & fn. 1 [individual actions that do not completely dispose of an employment discrimination cause of action cannot be summarily adjudicated].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(EPS/302)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories, And Demand For Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, October 1, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF BING HUANG's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories, And Demand For Production Of Documents (RP Associates LP). Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: 1830 17TH STREET LLC VS. SENTINEL INSURANCE COMPANY LTD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 1, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANT JEFFREY KAISER DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Jeffrey Kaiser's demurrer to the cause of action for negligent misrepresentation is overruled. In Bock v. Hansen (2014) 225 Cal.App.4th 215, the court held that "a cause of action for negligent misrepresentation can lie against an insurance adjuster." (Id. at 231.) Bock reasoned that an insurance adjuster is in a "special relationship" with the insureds, and that its conclusion is consistent with California law, which generally recognizes a cause of action for negligent misrepresentation in two circumstances: where providing false information poses a risk of and results in physical harm to person or property; and where information is conveyed in a commercial setting for a business purpose. (Id. at 229.) Bock parted company with prior California case authority holding that "[a]n independent adjuster engaged by an insurer owes no duty of care to the claimant insured, with whom the adjuster has no contract. The adjuster is not liable in tort to the insured for alleged negligent claims handling which causes only economic loss." (Sanchez v. Lindsey Morden Claims Servs., Inc. (1999) 72 Cal.App.4th 249, 250-251 [affirming order sustaining demurrer without leave to amend].) As the Sanchez court explained at length, there are good reasons for this general rule: "The insurer, not the adjuster, has the ultimate power to grant and deny coverage, and to pay the claim, delay it, or deny it. . . . [] An adjuster owes a duty to the insurer who engaged him. A new duty to the insured would conflict with that duty, and interfere with its faithful performance." (Id. at 253.) Further, the conclusion that no such duty should be imposed on adjusters is consistent with the law of agency, where the general rule is that agents are not liable to third parties for economic loss; the costs of imposing such a duty of care would outweigh the potential benefits; and doing so would significantly depart from existing California law and from the majority rule in other states, "which hold that an independent adjuster hired by the insurer owes no duty of care to the insured." (Id. at 253-256.) In the Court's view, Sanchez is the better-reasoned authority. However, Bock purported to distinguish Sanchez, and is binding on this Court. (See Auto Equity Sales, Inc. v. Superior Court (1962) 57 Cal.2d 450, 455.) Defendant's further argument that plaintiff has not adequately alleged a cause of action for negligent misrepresentation also fails in light of Bock. (See 225 Cal.App.4th at 231-232 [holding that insureds who alleged that adjuster falsely told them that their policy did not cover the cost of cleanup adequately alleged claim, and rejecting arguments that insureds did not justifiably rely on misrepresentation and did not sustain any damage].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(EPS/302)

rulings.Case Title: 1830 17TH STREET LLC VS. SENTINEL INSURANCE COMPANY LTD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 1, 2019, Line 10, Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint. (Part 2 of 2). A parent corporation may be liable: (1) where it is the alter ego of the subsidiary; (2) based on the representative services doctrine (subsidiary assists in parent's own business); and (3) agency (parent has pervasive control over subsidiary). (See DVI, Inc. v. Superior Court (2002) 104 Cal.App.4th 1080, 1093-1094.) A plaintiff relying on one of these theories of liability must make a sufficient evidentiary showing-and not merely rely on its allegations-to establish jurisdiction. (See DVI, Inc., 104 Cal.App.4th at 1094.) Here, plaintiff relies on a single e-mail from insurance adjuster Jeffrey Kaiser. (Mannion Dec., Ex. 1.) Plaintiff notes that the communication has a reference to HFSG under Mr. Kaiser's signature. This single piece of evidence fails to establish one of the adduced theories of liability and the court further notes that Mr. Kaiser signed the e-mail on behalf of Sentinel, not HFSG. In sum, plaintiff has failed to maintain its burden of proof and the motion to quash is granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for HFSG is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of tentative ruling). =(EPS/302)

rulings.Case Title: 1830 17TH STREET LLC VS. SENTINEL INSURANCE COMPANY LTD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION - Notice Of Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 1, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANT THE HARTFORD FINANCIAL SERVICES GROUP INC. MOTION - Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint. (Part 1 of 2) Hartford Financial Services Group, Inc.'s ("HFSG) motion to quash is granted. The parties agreed that HFSG could respond to the original complaint by July 24, 2019. Meanwhile, the parties met and conferred regarding dismissing "The Hartford" and HFSG and the filing of an amended complaint. Based on the parties' meet and confer efforts and the contemplated service of an amended complaint, it was not entirely clear when HFSG should have responded. (Austin Dec., Ex. 5, pg. 2 ["Unless plaintiff is willing to agree to dismiss HFSG now, what seems to make the most sense at this point is for plaintiff to go ahead and file the amended complaint dismissing The Hartford, and then we can continue to meet and confer as to HFSG based on all of our correspondence thus far."].) Based on these circumstances, the court finds that there is good cause to entertain the motion to quash even if it were technically filed late. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 418.10(a).) "When a nonresident defendant challenges personal jurisdiction the burden shifts to the plaintiff to demonstrate by a preponderance of the evidence that all necessary jurisdictional criteria are met. [citation] This burden must be met by competent evidence in affidavits and authenticated documentary evidence." (Ziller Electronics Lab GmbH v. Superior Court (1988) 206 Cal.App.3d 1222, 1232-1233; see also BBA Aviation PLC v. Superior Court (2010) 190 Cal.App.4th 421, 428-429 ["The plaintiff must do more than merely allege jurisdictional facts; plaintiff must provide affidavits and other authenticated documents demonstrating competent evidence of jurisdictional facts."].) In this case, plaintiff fails to present sufficient evidence to warrant a finding of general or specific jurisdiction. HFSG is a mere holding company that apparently does not conduct any business of its own. (Request for Judicial Notice, Ex. A, pg. 2.) HFSG is a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in Connecticut. The fact that it is the parent of Sentinel, which issued plaintiff's insurance policy, by itself is not a basis for jurisdiction. (See Daimler AG v. Bauman (2014) 571 U.S. 117, 134-135; F. Hoffman-La Roche, Ltd. v. Superior Court (2005) 130 Cal.App.4th 782, 802 ["Ownership or control of a subsidiary by a parent corporation, without more, is insufficient to subject the parent to jurisdiction in the state where the subsidiary does business."].). (Continued to Part 2) =(EPS/302)

rulings.Case Title: ACTIVE CONNEX INC. VS. ON DEMAND ICARS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 1, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANT ON DEMAND ICARS, INC. MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION. Defendant On Demand ICars' motion to compel arbitration and stay proceedings is granted. (See Code Civ. Proc., §§ 1281.2, 1281.4.) Plaintiff does not dispute that it entered into a valid arbitration agreement, but argues it has never refused to arbitrate and that Defendant waived its right to compel arbitration. A party seeking to compel arbitration must "plead and prove a prior demand for arbitration under the parties' arbitration agreement and a refusal to arbitrate under the agreement." (Mansouri v. Sup.Ct. (Fleur Du Lac Estates Ass'n) (2010) 181 Cal.App.4th 633, 830.) However, in certain circumstances, the opposing party's refusal to arbitrate may be demonstrated by its filing of a lawsuit rather than commencing arbitration proceedings, as required by the parties' agreement. (See Hyundai Amco America, Inc., v. S3H, Inc. (2014) 232 Cal.App.4th 572, 577 ["Arbitration can be refused without a formal demand ever having been made."].) Plaintiff's lawsuit against Defendant is sufficient to show a refusal to arbitrate the controversy. Plaintiff argues Defendant waived its right by refusing to initiate arbitration. Determination of waiver is a question of fact, not to be lightly inferred. (St. Agnes Medical Center v. PacifiCare of California (2003) 31 Cal.4th 1187, 1195.) Accordingly, the party who resists arbitration carries a heavy burden of proof and doubts regarding a waiver allegation should be resolved in favor of arbitration. (Id.) The Court finds Defendant did not waive its right to arbitration, but instead took the necessary steps to compel arbitration. (See Dial 800 v. Fesbinder (2004) 118 Cal.App.4th 32, 44 ["If a party wishes to compel arbitration, he must take active and decided steps to secure that right…the party seeking to enforce the contractual arbitration clause must file the section 1281.2 petition in the action at law (or raise it as an affirmative defense in the answer) or else the right to contractual arbitration is waived."].) Defendant filed an answer raising arbitrability of the claim as an affirmative defense and filed this motion when Plaintiff made known its plan to proceed with discovery. (See Lynn Decl. Ex. F.) In asserting that "it would be up to [Defendant] to initiate arbitration" (id.), Plaintiff has it exactly backwards: it was Plaintiff's obligation under the agreement to initiate arbitration by filing a demand for arbitration with the American Arbitration Association, rather than filing this lawsuit. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(EPS/302)

rulings.Case Title: JOEL BROWN VS. KIA MOTORS AMERICA INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer To Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 1, 2019, Line 13, Demurrer To Complaint. (Part 2 of 2). The demurrer to the sixth cause of action for fraud by omission is sustained without leave to amend. The economic loss rule bars the claim. Plaintiff alleges that Defendant concealed certain engine defects. However, Plaintiff does not plead that he suffered any damages other than the alleged economic damage related to the purchase of the vehicle itself. Plaintiff fails to plead any facts showing that he was exposed to liability for personal damages independent of the economic loss. "The economic loss rule requires a purchaser to recover in contract for purely economic loss due to disappointed expectations, unless he can demonstrate harm above and beyond a broken contractual promise." (Robinson Helicopter Co., Inc. v. Dana Corp. (2004) 34 Cal.4th 979, 988; Food Safety Net Services v. Eco Safe Systems USA, Inc. (2012) 209 Cal.App.4th 1118, 1132 [economic loss rule barred fraud claim based on falsehoods that were not conceptually distinct from the contract].) The "narrow" exception in Robinson Helicopter Co. does not apply because Plaintiff does not allege Defendant made any affirmative misrepresentations. (34 Cal.4th at 993 [court's holding was "limited to a defendant's affirmative misrepresentation on which a plaintiff relies and which expose a plaintiff to liability for personal damages independent of plaintiff's economic loss."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of tentative ruling). =(EPS/302)

rulings.Case Title: JOEL BROWN VS. KIA MOTORS AMERICA INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 1, 2019, Line 13, DEFENDANT KIA MOTORS AMERICA INC. DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. (Part 1 of 2). Defendant Kia Motors America, Inc.'s demurrer is overruled as to the third, fourth, and fifth causes of action. The demurrer to the sixth cause of action is sustained without leave to amend. The demurrer as to the third cause of action is overruled. Liberally construing the allegations, Plaintiff has pled sufficient facts to allege a violation of Civil Code § 1793.2(a). The demurrer to the fourth cause of action for breach of written express warranty is overruled. The complaint alleges sufficient facts to establish a claim. (See Compl. 10-11, 46-48.) The demurrer to the fifth cause of action for breach of implied warranty of merchantability is overruled. Under the Song-Beverly Act, with limited exceptions not applicable here every retail sale of "consumer goods" in California contains an implied warranty of merchantability. (Civ. Code § 1792.) The implied warranty of merchantability may be breached by a latent defect undiscoverable at the time of sale. (Mexia v. Rinker Boat Co., Inc. (2009) 174 Cal.App.4th 1297, 1304.) Defendant argues that a breach of implied warranty occurred when Plaintiff purchased the vehicle in August 2012 and that the claim is barred by the four-year statute of limitations. However, the Court finds the "future performance" exception applies here. (Cal. U. Com. Code § 2725(2) ["A breach of warranty occurs when tender of delivery is made, except that where a warranty explicitly extends to future performance of the goods and discovery of the breach must await the time of such performance the cause of action accrues when the breach is or sohould have been discovered."]; see Cardinal Health 301, Inc. v. Tyco Electronics Corp. (2008) 169 Cal.App.4th 116, 129 ["Parties must make a 'clear and unambiguous expression' of their intent to extend warranties to cover future performance."].) Here, Plaintiff had express warranties, including a 5-year/60,000 mile express bumper to bumper warranty, a 10-year/100,000 mile powertrain warranty which covered the engine and transmission, and a 10-year/120,000 mile warranty extension on the Subject Vehicle. (Compl. 10.) These warranties cannot be ignored, as they explicitly extend to the future performance of the vehicle. Thus, the statute of limitations did not run until Plaintiff became aware of the defect in March-April 2019. (Compl. 61; see Ehrlich v. BMW of North America (C.D. Cal. 2010) 801 F.Supp.2d 908, 925 [plaintiff's warranty tolled the statute of limitations until plaintiff reasonably knew that his MINI would not perform as it should, which did not occur until his windshield cracked and BMW would not replace it.].). (Continued to Part 2) =(EPS/302)

rulings.Case Title: JOEL BROWN VS. KIA MOTORS AMERICA INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 1, 2019, Line 13, DEFENDANT KIA MOTORS AMERICA INC. MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendant Kia Motors America, Inc.'s motion to strike plaintiff's prayer for punitive damages is granted. Punitive damages are not recoverable on Plaintiff's sixth cause of action for fraud, as the Court has sustained Defendant's demurrer to that cause of action without leave to amend. Moreover, the Song-Beverly Act does not authorize a court to award punitive damages. (See Civ. Code § 1794.) None of the cases cited by Plaintiff, all of which addressed the distinct civil penalty provision of the Act (id. § 1794(c)), holds to the contrary. Defendant's motion to strike Plaintiff's prayer for civil penalties for a violation of Civil Code § 1793.2(a)(3) (Compl. 98) is denied. The Song-Beverly Act expressly authorizes a civil penalty for a willful failure to comply with "any obligation under this chapter or under an implied or express warranty or service contract." (Civ. Code § 1794(c); see Schreidel v. American Honda Motor Co. (1995) 34 Cal.App.4th 1242, 1249-1250.) Defendant ignores this provision. Plaintiff has alleged that Defendant willfully failed to comply with its statutory obligations. Defendant's motion to strike Plaintiff's prayer for prejudgment interest is denied. Defendant fails to support that motion with any authority or argument, and it raises a factual issue (whether plaintiff's damages are reasonably capable of being made certain) that cannot be determined on the face of the complaint.
